ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for mixed vegetables with tunghoon (glass noodles). Closest defensible match is the stir-fried vegetable dish class. Tunghoon (mung bean vermicelli) is a noodle component not separately coded in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Stir-fried mixed vegetables with noodles — FoodEx2 composite dish category for cooked vegetable and noodle preparations
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (mixed cooked vegetable dish) used as closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for tunghoon-based preparations. Composite dish with glass noodles, carrot, cabbage, and dried prawn.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Singaporean mixed vegetable and glass noodle dish. SNOMED CT does not provide granular codes for regional composite vegetable dishes of this type.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mixed vegetables with tunghoon is approximately 200g, equivalent to one medium plate as served at a Singapore hawker centre or home meal.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Vegetables with Tunghoon — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mixed Vegetables with Tunghoon (cr_id F14080020). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from HPB vegetable stir-fry category data as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; this field is marked as an approximation.

gi_source.title: Mung bean vermicelli (glass noodles) GI — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to mixed vegetable dish with tunghoon
gi_source.source_match_notes: Tunghoon (dong fen) is mung bean vermicelli / glass noodles. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of approximately 26 (low) for mung bean vermicelli / glass noodles (boiled). The dish contains 13.76g carbohydrate per 100g. For a 200g serving (27.5g available carbohydrate), glycemic load = (26 × 27.5) / 100 = 7.15, rounded to 7.2. The vegetable components (carrot, cabbage) and fibre (2.0g/100g) further moderate the glycaemic response. The input record's GI of 15 with confidence 0.45 from an 'Embedded GI Database' (non-approved source) has been replaced with this approved-source analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 224mg/100g; borderline sodium content with limited potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150g and request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce during preparation, as restaurant versions may contain significantly higher sodium than the 224mg/100g reference value; home preparation with low-sodium soy sauce is strongly preferred.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (26) from mung bean glass noodles; moderate carbohydrate at 13.76g/100g with 2.0g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mixed vegetables with tunghoon is suitable for people with type 2 diabetes due to the low glycaemic index of mung bean glass noodles (GI ~26) and the fibre and micronutrient contribution of the vegetable components; limit serving to 150–200g and pair with a lean protein source to further moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat (4.2g/100g) and borderline sodium (224mg/100g); low saturated fat and high vegetable content are favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish's high vegetable content, low sugar (0.65g/100g), and low saturated fat profile are favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the sodium content of 224mg/100g warrants caution — individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sodium preparation and limit portion to one moderate serving (150–200g) to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory vegetable components (cabbage, carrot) providing sulforaphane, beta-carotene, and vitamin C
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The cruciferous vegetable base (cabbage) and carrot provide anti-inflammatory phytonutrients including sulforaphane and beta-carotene; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced soy sauce to minimise sodium-related tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Vegetables with Tunghoon (杂菜冬粉, Za Cai Dong Fen) is a popular Singaporean Chinese stir-fried dish combining tunghoon — mung bean glass noodles (also called dong fen or cellophane noodles) — with carrot, cabbage, and dried prawn, seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and garlic. The dish is cooked by stir-frying the vegetables and rehydrated glass noodles together in a wok over high heat, resulting in a lightly sauced, tender-textured preparation with a savoury umami flavour profile. Tunghoon (冬粉) are translucent, slippery noodles made from mung bean starch that absorb the cooking sauce well and have a characteristically soft yet slightly springy texture. Per 100g, the dish provides 99 kcal, 1.5g protein, 4.2g fat, 13.76g carbohydrate, 0.65g sugar, 2.0g dietary fibre, and 224mg sodium. The glycaemic index is low (approximately 26, analogous to mung bean vermicelli per the University of Sydney GI Database), making it a moderate-glycaemic dish suitable for people managing blood glucose. The vegetable components contribute antioxidants including beta-carotene from carrot and sulforaphane from cabbage. Sodium at 224mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management and may be higher in restaurant preparations. The dish is commonly served as a side dish or light main at Singapore hawker centres, coffee shops, and home meals, and is widely available across Chinese-Singaporean food stalls. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the dried prawn content, and contains soy and shellfish allergens.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080020) with confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mung bean vermicelli (GI ~26), which is the primary ingredient tunghoon. Saturated fat is approximated from HPB vegetable stir-fry category data. All other fields are complete and consistent with the approved source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.6g/100g) is approximated from HPB Singapore vegetable stir-fry category data, as the HPB F14080020 record does not separately report saturated fat breakdown; this value should be verified against a full fatty acid panel if available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish (F14080020) and are not available in an approved secondary source for this specific preparation. 3. The input record's GI value of 15 with confidence 0.45 from 'Embedded GI Database' has been replaced with an approved-source analogous estimate of GI 26 (low) from the University of Sydney GI Database for mung bean vermicelli, which is the primary carbohydrate source (tunghoon). 4. The dish contains dried prawn and is therefore not suitable for vegetarians or vegans despite the vegetable-dominant composition; the input record's dietary_tags of 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' have been corrected to false. 5. Allergens include crustaceans (dried prawn), soy (soy sauce), and molluscs (oyster sauce). 6. Halal status is not confirmed as oyster sauce and dried prawn sourcing requires certification verification. 7. FoodEx2 code A0FJP is used as the closest defensible composite dish anchor; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific Singaporean preparation.
